skill.name: pattern-detection
skill.description: Detect code smells, vulnerability patterns, sensitive data patterns, anomalies, and trends using read-only search and analysis workflows.
skill.summary: Find recurring code, data, and security patterns with read-only analysis guidance.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static command, network, environment, weak-crypto, and dangerous-combination findings were reviewed in context. Most are false positives from Markdown examples and reference links, but the secret-scanning example can print matched secret values if used without redaction.

medium_findings.title: Secret Pattern Search Can Expose Matched Values
medium_findings.description: The hard-coded secret search uses grep patterns that normally print the full matching line. This is useful for auditing, but it can expose passwords, API keys, or cloud keys in the model transcript unless results are redacted.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The grep examples directly search for password, secret, api_key, and AWS key patterns. The skill also says not to log sensitive information, so the risk is exposure through normal grep output rather than confirmed malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Read-Only Shell Examples Trigger Static Command Alerts
low_findings.description: The external command findings are Markdown examples for grep, awk, cut, sort, and wc. The skill metadata only allows read-oriented tools, and the commands are aimed at local pattern detection rather than modification or exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content appears inside fenced documentation examples with read-only intent. Some examples could still be run by an assistant with shell access, so a low operational risk remains.

low_findings.title: Reference URLs Are Not Network Behavior
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to external documentation references. No code or instruction sends data to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are listed under References and are not used in any request, fetch, curl, webhook, or upload instruction.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto And Env-Access Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts do not correspond to cryptographic code in the inspected skill text. The env-access alert is a regex example that searches source files for API key assignments, not access to runtime environment variables.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No weak crypto implementation or environment variable access was found at the cited lines. The cited content is descriptive text, report formatting, or a search pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides shell snippets for local searches. They are mostly read-only, but marketplaces should display that this skill can lead assistants to suggest command-line scanning workflows.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit in the skill instructions, but the allowed tools field limits intended execution to read-oriented tools.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Data Match Reporting
dangerous_patterns.description: The secret and sensitive-data examples can identify credentials and personal data. Reports should avoid including raw matched values.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly includes password, API key, AWS key, credit card, and SSN pattern examples. The risk is bounded by local analysis and the stated prohibition on logging sensitive information.

content.value_statement: Teams need a fast way to find repeated code issues, risky patterns, and unusual data changes. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through read-only pattern detection and reporting.
actual_capabilities: Identifies long functions, duplicate checks, magic numbers, TODO markers, and empty error handlers.
Suggests read-only searches for SQL injection patterns, dangerous functions, and hard-coded secrets.
Provides regex examples for emails, phone numbers, IP addresses, payment card patterns, and SSN patterns.
Explains z-score, IQR, moving average, growth rate, and volatility analysis for datasets.
Defines a structured report format with severity, file, line, pattern, and recommended action fields.
limitations: Pattern matches can produce false positives and need human review.
The skill does not automatically modify code or apply fixes.
Secret scanning examples must be used with redaction to avoid exposing sensitive values.
Statistical examples require suitable data and appropriate Python libraries.

use_cases.title: Review a Pull Request
use_cases.description: Find repeated code smells, risky constructs, and unfinished work before a reviewer spends time on manual inspection.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Security Sweep
use_cases.description: Create a focused list of files that may contain injection risks, dangerous calls, or hard-coded credentials.

use_cases.title: Analyze Product Metrics
use_cases.description: Detect outliers, moving-average shifts, growth changes, and volatility in time-series data.

prompt_templates.title: Find Basic Code Smells
prompt_templates.prompt: Use pattern-detection to review this repository for long functions, magic numbers, TODO markers, and empty error handling. Return a concise report with files, line numbers, severity, and recommended actions.

prompt_templates.title: Scan for Security Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Use pattern-detection to look for SQL injection patterns, dangerous eval or exec usage, innerHTML assignments, and possible hard-coded secrets. Redact any sensitive matched values and explain false-positive risks.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Pattern Frequency
prompt_templates.prompt: Use pattern-detection to compare recurring patterns across modules. Group the results by pattern type, affected area, likely root cause, and priority for cleanup.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Trends and Anomalies
prompt_templates.prompt: Use pattern-detection to analyze this dataset for outliers, moving-average changes, growth shifts, and volatility. Explain the method used and note data quality assumptions.

output_examples.input: Review a TypeScript service for code smells and risky patterns.
output: Summary: 42 files reviewed, 9 patterns found, 2 high priority items.
High: Possible hard-coded API key assignment in a configuration file. Value redacted.
Medium: Three functions exceed the agreed length threshold.
Recommended action: Verify each match, redact secrets, and create focused cleanup tasks.

output_examples.input: Analyze weekly sign-up metrics for anomalies and trend changes.
output: Summary: Two statistical outliers were detected in the last quarter.
Trend: Seven-day moving average is above the thirty-day moving average.
Risk: One spike aligns with missing source attribution and needs data validation.

best_practices: Start with narrow patterns and expand only after checking sample matches.
Redact credentials, personal data, and payment data before sharing results.
Separate confirmed issues from likely false positives in every report.
anti_patterns: Do not treat every regex match as a confirmed defect.
Do not publish raw secret or personal-data matches in reports.
Do not use broad repository scans when a focused file set is available.

faq.question: Does this skill change code?
faq.answer: No. The skill is designed for read-only detection, reporting, and prioritization.

faq.question: Can it find security vulnerabilities?
faq.answer: It can identify common risky patterns, but a human should confirm each finding.

faq.question: Does it expose secrets?
faq.answer: It can find secret-like patterns. Users should redact matched values before reporting.

faq.question: Can it analyze data trends?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for z-score, IQR, moving averages, growth, and volatility.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: No. The inspected skill only links to reference sites and does not send data externally.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
